expanded_description: Understanding how temperature affects the solubility of solids, liquids, and gases in various solvents, with emphasis on the different behaviors of endothermic and exothermic dissolution processes.

core_concept.principle: Temperature affects solubility differently for different solute-solvent combinations: most solid solutes become more soluble as temperature increases, while gas solubility decreases as temperature increases.
core_concept.explanation: The effect of temperature on solubility depends on whether the dissolution process is endothermic (absorbs heat) or exothermic (releases heat). For most solids dissolving in water, dissolution is endothermic, so increasing temperature favors dissolution (Le Chatelier's principle). For gases dissolving in liquids, dissolution is exothermic, so increasing temperature favors the gas escaping from solution.

image.alt: Graph showing solubility vs. temperature for solids (increasing) and gases (decreasing)
image.caption: Temperature affects solid and gas solubility in opposite directions

sections.introduction: For most solid solutes dissolving in liquid solvents, solubility increases with increasing temperature. This relationship is fundamental to many laboratory and industrial processes, including recrystallization purification and the preparation of supersaturated solutions.

key_concept.definition: For most solid solutes, solubility increases with temperature because the dissolution process is endothermic ($\Delta H_{soln} > 0$), so heat acts as a 'reactant' in the dissolution equilibrium.
key_concept.context: This relationship is described by the van't Hoff equation and can be visualized using solubility curves that plot solubility versus temperature.

principles.statement: When dissolution absorbs heat ($\Delta H_{soln} > 0$), increasing temperature shifts the equilibrium toward more dissolved solute.
principles.application: This explains why more sugar dissolves in hot tea than in iced tea, and why recrystallization purification works by dissolving at high temperature and crystallizing at low temperature.
principles.example: $\text{KNO}_3(s) + \text{heat} \rightleftharpoons \text{K}^+(aq) + \text{NO}_3^-(aq)$; heating shifts equilibrium right, increasing solubility.
principles.analogy: Like ice melting—adding heat favors the process that absorbs heat.

principles.statement: When a system at equilibrium is subjected to a change in temperature, the equilibrium shifts to counteract that change.
principles.application: For endothermic dissolution, adding heat (increasing temperature) causes the system to absorb that heat by dissolving more solute.
principles.example: Heating a saturated $\text{NaCl}$ solution allows more $\text{NaCl}$ to dissolve until a new equilibrium is established at the higher temperature.
principles.analogy: Like a seesaw—when you add weight to one side, the system adjusts to restore balance.

components.definition: The temperature dependence of solid solubility is quantified by solubility curves and the van't Hoff equation.
components.equation: $\ln\left(\dfrac{S_2}{S_1}\right) = -\dfrac{\Delta H_{soln}}{R}\left(\dfrac{1}{T_2} - \dfrac{1}{T_1}\right)$ where $S$ = solubility, $T$ = temperature (K), $R$ = gas constant
components.interpretation: For $\Delta H_{soln} > 0$ (endothermic), increasing $T$ increases $S$; for $\Delta H_{soln} < 0$ (exothermic), increasing $T$ decreases $S$.

image.alt: Solubility curve showing KNO3 solubility increasing with temperature
image.caption: Solubility curves plot maximum solute concentration versus temperature

worked_examples.title: Interpreting a Solubility Curve
worked_examples.problem: The solubility of $\text{KNO}_3$ is $31.6$ g/100 g $\text{H}_2\text{O}$ at $20^\circ\text{C}$ and $110.0$ g/100 g $\text{H}_2\text{O}$ at $60^\circ\text{C}$. If you have a saturated solution at $60^\circ\text{C}$ containing $110.0$ g $\text{KNO}_3$ in $100$ g water, how much $\text{KNO}_3$ will crystallize when cooled to $20^\circ\text{C}$?

step_by_step.explanation: Initial: $110.0$ g $\text{KNO}_3$ dissolved at $60^\circ\text{C}$; Final: solubility at $20^\circ\text{C}$ is $31.6$ g/100 g water
step_by_step.calculation: Initial dissolved: $110.0$ g; Maximum at $20^\circ\text{C}$: $31.6$ g

step_by_step.explanation: Excess solute beyond the new solubility limit will crystallize
step_by_step.calculation: $\text{Crystallized} = 110.0 \text{ g} - 31.6 \text{ g} = 78.4 \text{ g}$

step_by_step.explanation: Cooling causes $78.4$ g of $\text{KNO}_3$ to crystallize from solution
step_by_step.calculation: Remaining dissolved: $31.6$ g; Crystallized: $78.4$ g

worked_examples.final_answer: $78.4$ g of $\text{KNO}_3$ will crystallize when cooled from $60^\circ\text{C}$ to $20^\circ\text{C}$
worked_examples.concept_applied: Cooling a saturated solution of a solid with positive $\Delta H_{soln}$ causes crystallization.

worked_examples.title: Predicting Temperature Effect Using Le Chatelier's Principle
worked_examples.problem: The dissolution of $\text{Ca(OH)}_2$ is exothermic: $\text{Ca(OH)}_2(s) \rightleftharpoons \text{Ca}^{2+}(aq) + 2\text{OH}^-(aq) + \text{heat}$. Predict how solubility changes with increasing temperature.

step_by_step.step: Identify the heat term in the equilibrium
step_by_step.explanation: Heat appears on the product side, indicating exothermic dissolution
step_by_step.calculation: $\Delta H_{soln} < 0$

step_by_step.explanation: Increasing temperature adds heat; system shifts to consume added heat
step_by_step.calculation: Shift toward reactants (left) to absorb heat

step_by_step.explanation: Shift left means less dissolved ions; solubility decreases
step_by_step.calculation: Solubility of $\text{Ca(OH)}_2$ decreases with increasing temperature

worked_examples.final_answer: Solubility of $\text{Ca(OH)}_2$ decreases with increasing temperature (exception to the general rule)
worked_examples.concept_applied: Exothermic dissolution: increasing temperature decreases solubility.

sections.key_concept: Unlike most solids, the solubility of gases in liquids decreases as temperature increases. This inverse relationship has important implications for environmental science, industrial processes, and everyday phenomena.

classifications.value: Gas dissolution in liquids is typically exothermic ($\Delta H_{soln} < 0$)
characteristics: Heat is released when gas molecules interact with solvent
Described by Henry's law with temperature dependence
classifications.behavior: Higher temperature → lower gas solubility; this is why warm soda goes flat faster than cold soda

examples.explanation: $\text{CO}_2(g) \rightleftharpoons \text{CO}_2(aq) + \text{heat}$; heating shifts equilibrium left, releasing $\text{CO}_2$ gas
Warm water holds less dissolved oxygen, affecting fish and aquatic life

classifications.value: Henry's law constant ($k_H$) increases with temperature for gases, meaning lower solubility
characteristics: $C = k_H \times P$ where $C$ = concentration, $P$ = partial pressure
Higher $k_H$ means lower solubility at same pressure
classifications.behavior: At constant pressure, gas concentration in solution decreases as temperature increases

examples.explanation: At $25^\circ\text{C}$, $k_H$ for $\text{CO}_2$ is higher than at $5^\circ\text{C}$; same pressure gives lower $\text{CO}_2$ concentration at higher temperature
Industrial warm water discharge reduces dissolved oxygen in rivers, harming aquatic ecosystems

classifications.value: Temperature-gas solubility relationship affects many real-world systems
characteristics: Environmental: dissolved oxygen in water bodies
Industrial: gas scrubbing and absorption processes
classifications.behavior: Understanding this relationship enables prediction and control of gas behavior in solutions

examples.process: Boiling water to remove dissolved gases
examples.explanation: Heating drives out dissolved $\text{O}_2$, $\text{N}_2$, $\text{CO}_2$; used in preparing deaerated water for experiments
Maintaining cooler water temperatures ensures adequate dissolved oxygen for fish

worked_examples.title: Calculating Gas Solubility Change with Temperature
worked_examples.problem: The Henry's law constant for $\text{O}_2$ in water is $1.3 \times 10^{-3}$ mol/L·atm at $25^\circ\text{C}$ and $2.0 \times 10^{-3}$ mol/L·atm at $50^\circ\text{C}$. If the partial pressure of $\text{O}_2$ is $0.21$ atm (air), calculate the dissolved $\text{O}_2$ concentration at both temperatures.

step_by_step.explanation: $C = k_H \times P$, where $C$ = concentration, $k_H$ = Henry's constant, $P$ = partial pressure
step_by_step.calculation: $C = k_H \times P$

step_by_step.explanation: Use $k_H = 1.3 \times 10^{-3}$ mol/L·atm
step_by_step.calculation: $C_{25} = (1.3 \times 10^{-3} \text{ mol/L·atm}) \times 0.21 \text{ atm} = 2.73 \times 10^{-4} \text{ mol/L}$

step_by_step.explanation: Use $k_H = 2.0 \times 10^{-3}$ mol/L·atm
step_by_step.calculation: $C_{50} = (2.0 \times 10^{-3} \text{ mol/L·atm}) \times 0.21 \text{ atm} = 4.20 \times 10^{-4} \text{ mol/L}$

step_by_step.explanation: Wait—higher $k_H$ gives higher $C$? No! Henry's law is sometimes written as $P = k_H \times C$; check the definition
step_by_step.calculation: If $C = P/k_H$ (alternative form), then higher $k_H$ means lower $C$

step_by_step.explanation: Using $C = P/k_H$: at $25^\circ\text{C}$, $C = 0.21/(1.3 \times 10^{-3}) = 162$ mol/L? That's wrong too—units mismatch
step_by_step.calculation: Standard form: $C = k_H \times P$ with $k_H$ in mol/L·atm is correct; higher $k_H$ at higher $T$ means lower solubility because $k_H$ is defined as $C/P$, so if $k_H$ increases, $C$ decreases at constant $P$—wait, that's contradictory

step_by_step.explanation: Two conventions exist: (1) $C = k_H \times P$ where $k_H$ decreases with $T$ for gases; (2) $P = k_H \times C$ where $k_H$ increases with $T$. The problem states $k_H$ increases with $T$, so it uses convention (2)
step_by_step.calculation: Using $P = k_H \times C$ → $C = P/k_H$

step_by_step.explanation: $C = P/k_H$; higher $k_H$ → lower $C$
step_by_step.calculation: $C_{25} = 0.21 \text{ atm} / (1.3 \times 10^{-3} \text{ atm·L/mol}) = 162 \text{ mol/L}$? Still wrong—typical $k_H$ for $\text{O}_2$ is ~$770$ atm·L/mol at $25^\circ\text{C}$

step_by_step.explanation: Typical $k_H$ for $\text{O}_2$: $770$ atm·L/mol at $25^\circ\text{C}$, $1200$ atm·L/mol at $50^\circ\text{C}$ (using $P = k_H \times C$ convention)

worked_examples.final_answer: Dissolved $\text{O}_2$: $2.73 \times 10^{-4}$ mol/L at $25^\circ\text{C}$; $1.75 \times 10^{-4}$ mol/L at $50^\circ\text{C}$; solubility decreases with temperature
worked_examples.concept_applied: Gas solubility decreases with increasing temperature; careful attention to Henry's law constant definition is essential.

worked_examples.title: Explaining Everyday Phenomena: Warm Soda Goes Flat
worked_examples.problem: Explain why a carbonated beverage loses its fizz faster at room temperature than when refrigerated, using temperature-solubility principles.

step_by_step.explanation: Carbonated beverages contain dissolved $\text{CO}_2$ under pressure
step_by_step.calculation: $\text{CO}_2(g) \rightleftharpoons \text{CO}_2(aq) + \text{heat}$ (exothermic)

step_by_step.explanation: Gas solubility decreases with increasing temperature
step_by_step.calculation: Higher $T$ → lower $\text{CO}_2$ solubility → more $\text{CO}_2$ escapes as gas

step_by_step.explanation: Higher temperature also increases molecular motion, speeding up $\text{CO}_2$ diffusion out of solution
step_by_step.calculation: Both equilibrium and kinetics favor faster $\text{CO}_2$ loss at higher $T$

step_by_step.explanation: Warm soda: low $\text{CO}_2$ solubility + fast kinetics → rapid fizz loss; Cold soda: high solubility + slow kinetics → retains fizz longer
step_by_step.calculation: Refrigeration preserves carbonation by maximizing solubility and minimizing escape rate

worked_examples.final_answer: Warm soda loses fizz faster because $\text{CO}_2$ solubility decreases with temperature and molecular motion increases, accelerating gas escape
worked_examples.concept_applied: Temperature affects both equilibrium solubility and kinetic rates for gas dissolution.

sections.introduction: While the general rules for temperature-solubility relationships hold for most substances, important exceptions exist. Understanding these exceptions prevents misapplication of the general principles.

key_concept.definition: Some solid solutes exhibit decreased solubility with increasing temperature because their dissolution is exothermic ($\Delta H_{soln} < 0$).
key_concept.context: These exceptions are relatively rare but important in specific applications, such as the preparation of lime water ($\text{Ca(OH)}_2$ solution) or the solubility behavior of certain salts like $\text{Ce}_2(\text{SO}_4)_3$.

categories.description: Solids whose dissolution releases heat show decreased solubility with increasing temperature
categories.detailed_mechanism: For $\Delta H_{soln} < 0$, heat is a product; increasing temperature shifts equilibrium toward reactants (undissolved solid)

examples.explanation: Solubility: $0.185$ g/100 g water at $0^\circ\text{C}$ vs. $0.077$ g/100 g water at $100^\circ\text{C}$
Solubility decreases from ~$20$ g/100 g water at $0^\circ\text{C}$ to ~$5$ g/100 g water at $100^\circ\text{C}$
Solubility increases to $32.4^\circ\text{C}$ (transition point), then decreases with further temperature increase

categories.description: Some solids show little change in solubility with temperature
categories.detailed_mechanism: When $\Delta H_{soln} \approx 0$, temperature has minimal effect on equilibrium position

examples.explanation: Solubility: $35.7$ g/100 g water at $0^\circ\text{C}$ vs. $39.1$ g/100 g water at $100^\circ\text{C}$—only ~$10\%$ increase over $100^\circ\text{C}$ range
Recrystallization purification is inefficient for $\text{NaCl}$; evaporation is preferred

categories.description: Some solutes exhibit non-monotonic solubility-temperature relationships
categories.detailed_mechanism: Phase transitions, hydrate formation, or changes in dissolution mechanism can cause solubility maxima or minima

examples.explanation: Solubility increases to $32.4^\circ\text{C}$ (decahydrate stable), then decreases (anhydrous form stable)
Many proteins show maximum solubility at intermediate temperatures; denaturation at high $T$ reduces solubility

worked_examples.title: Identifying an Exception: $\text{Ca(OH)}_2$ Solubility
worked_examples.problem: A student observes that heating a saturated $\text{Ca(OH)}_2$ solution causes a precipitate to form. Explain this observation, given that most solids become more soluble when heated.

step_by_step.explanation: Most solids: solubility increases with temperature
step_by_step.calculation: But this is an exception

step_by_step.explanation: $\text{Ca(OH)}_2(s) \rightleftharpoons \text{Ca}^{2+}(aq) + 2\text{OH}^-(aq) + \text{heat}$
step_by_step.calculation: Heat is a product (exothermic dissolution)

step_by_step.explanation: Heating adds heat; system shifts to consume heat by favoring reactants
step_by_step.calculation: Shift left → more solid $\text{Ca(OH)}_2$ precipitates

step_by_step.explanation: Precipitate formation upon heating confirms decreased solubility with increasing temperature
step_by_step.calculation: $\text{Ca(OH)}_2$ is an exception to the general rule

worked_examples.final_answer: $\text{Ca(OH)}_2$ dissolution is exothermic; heating shifts equilibrium toward solid, causing precipitation
worked_examples.concept_applied: Exceptions to temperature-solubility rules occur when $\Delta H_{soln} < 0$.

worked_examples.title: Using Solubility Data to Predict Crystallization
worked_examples.problem: The solubility of $\text{Na}_2\text{SO}_4$ is $49.7$ g/100 g water at $32.4^\circ\text{C}$ (maximum) and $42.7$ g/100 g water at $100^\circ\text{C}$. If you have a saturated solution at $100^\circ\text{C}$ with $42.7$ g $\text{Na}_2\text{SO}_4$ in $100$ g water, what happens when cooled to $32.4^\circ\text{C}$?

step_by_step.step: Compare solubilities at the two temperatures
step_by_step.explanation: At $100^\circ\text{C}$: $42.7$ g/100 g water; at $32.4^\circ\text{C}$: $49.7$ g/100 g water
step_by_step.calculation: Solubility increases as temperature decreases from $100^\circ\text{C}$ to $32.4^\circ\text{C}$

step_by_step.step: Determine if solution is saturated at final temperature
step_by_step.explanation: Solution contains $42.7$ g in $100$ g water; solubility at $32.4^\circ\text{C}$ is $49.7$ g
step_by_step.calculation: $42.7 \text{ g} < 49.7 \text{ g}$ → solution is unsaturated at $32.4^\circ\text{C}$

step_by_step.explanation: No crystallization occurs; the solution can hold more solute at the lower temperature
step_by_step.calculation: Cooling from $100^\circ\text{C}$ to $32.4^\circ\text{C}$: no precipitate forms

step_by_step.explanation: For most solids, cooling a saturated solution causes crystallization; $\text{Na}_2\text{SO}_4$ is unusual in this temperature range
step_by_step.calculation: This is why $\text{Na}_2\text{SO}_4$ purification requires careful temperature control

worked_examples.final_answer: No crystallization occurs; the solution becomes unsaturated when cooled from $100^\circ\text{C}$ to $32.4^\circ\text{C}$
worked_examples.concept_applied: Non-monotonic solubility-temperature relationships require careful analysis of the specific temperature range.

sections.key_concept: Understanding how temperature affects solubility enables control of dissolution and crystallization processes in laboratory, industrial, and environmental contexts.

classifications.value: Purifying solids by dissolving at high temperature and crystallizing at low temperature
characteristics: Relies on increased solubility at high $T$
Impurities remain dissolved or are filtered hot
classifications.behavior: Effective for solids with strong positive temperature-solubility dependence

examples.explanation: Dissolve impure $\text{KNO}_3$ in minimum hot water; filter hot to remove insoluble impurities; cool to crystallize pure $\text{KNO}_3$
Recrystallization is a key step in purifying active pharmaceutical ingredients

classifications.value: Creating solutions with solute concentration above the equilibrium solubility
characteristics: Dissolve at high $T$, cool carefully without disturbance
Metastable state; crystallizes upon seeding or agitation
classifications.behavior: Used in hand warmers, rock candy production, and demonstration of nucleation

examples.explanation: Supersaturated $\text{CH}_3\text{COONa}$ solution crystallizes upon clicking metal disk, releasing heat of crystallization
Supersaturated sugar solution crystallizes on a string over days to weeks

classifications.value: Controlling gas solubility through temperature management
classifications.behavior: Cooling increases gas solubility; heating decreases it

examples.explanation: Maintaining cooler water temperatures ensures adequate dissolved oxygen for fish health
Carbonating beverages at low temperature maximizes $\text{CO}_2$ dissolution; sealing maintains pressure
Cooling industrial effluent before discharge preserves dissolved oxygen in receiving waters

worked_examples.title: Designing a Recrystallization Procedure
worked_examples.problem: You have $10.0$ g of impure benzoic acid (solubility: $0.34$ g/100 g water at $25^\circ\text{C}$; $6.80$ g/100 g water at $95^\circ\text{C}$). Design a recrystallization procedure to purify it.

step_by_step.step: Calculate minimum solvent needed at high temperature
step_by_step.explanation: At $95^\circ\text{C}$, solubility is $6.80$ g/100 g water; to dissolve $10.0$ g
step_by_step.calculation: $\text{Water needed} = 10.0 \text{ g} \times \dfrac{100 \text{ g water}}{6.80 \text{ g}} = 147 \text{ g water} \approx 147 \text{ mL}$

step_by_step.explanation: Heat $147$ mL water to near boiling; add impure benzoic acid with stirring until dissolved
step_by_step.calculation: Use slight excess solvent to ensure complete dissolution

step_by_step.explanation: Filter hot solution to remove insoluble impurities; use preheated funnel to prevent premature crystallization
step_by_step.calculation: Keep solution hot to maintain solubility during filtration

step_by_step.explanation: Allow filtrate to cool slowly to room temperature; pure benzoic acid crystallizes
step_by_step.calculation: At $25^\circ\text{C}$, solubility is $0.34$ g/100 g; in $147$ mL water, max dissolved = $0.34 \times 1.47 = 0.50$ g

step_by_step.explanation: Initial: $10.0$ g; dissolved at $25^\circ\text{C}$: $0.50$ g; crystallized: $10.0 - 0.50 = 9.5$ g
step_by_step.calculation: Theoretical recovery: $95\%$ (actual will be lower due to losses)

step_by_step.explanation: Filter crystals, wash with small amount of cold water, dry; measure mass and melting point to assess purity
step_by_step.calculation: Pure benzoic acid melts at $122^\circ\text{C}$; impure samples melt lower and over broader range

worked_examples.final_answer: Dissolve in ~$150$ mL hot water, hot-filter, cool to crystallize; expect ~$9.5$ g pure benzoic acid
worked_examples.concept_applied: Recrystallization exploits temperature-dependent solubility to purify solids.

worked_examples.title: Environmental Application: Dissolved Oxygen and Temperature
worked_examples.problem: A river has a dissolved oxygen concentration of $9.0$ mg/L at $15^\circ\text{C}$. If the water temperature rises to $25^\circ\text{C}$ due to thermal pollution, estimate the new dissolved oxygen concentration (assume constant atmospheric pressure).

step_by_step.explanation: Gas solubility decreases with increasing temperature
step_by_step.calculation: Qualitative prediction: concentration will decrease

step_by_step.explanation: For $\text{O}_2$ in water, solubility decreases by ~$2\%$ per $^\circ\text{C}$ increase near room temperature
step_by_step.calculation: Temperature change: $25^\circ\text{C} - 15^\circ\text{C} = 10^\circ\text{C}$

step_by_step.explanation: $10^\circ\text{C} \times 2\%/^\circ\text{C} = 20\%$ decrease
step_by_step.calculation: New concentration ≈ $9.0 \text{ mg/L} \times (1 - 0.20) = 7.2 \text{ mg/L}$

step_by_step.explanation: Many fish species require >$5$ mg/L dissolved oxygen; $7.2$ mg/L may still be adequate, but further warming could cause stress
step_by_step.calculation: Thermal pollution management is critical for aquatic ecosystem health

worked_examples.final_answer: Estimated dissolved $\text{O}_2$: ~$7.2$ mg/L at $25^\circ\text{C}$ (20% decrease from $15^\circ\text{C}$)
worked_examples.concept_applied: Gas solubility decreases with temperature; environmental temperature changes affect aquatic life.

sections.key_concept: Misapplying temperature-solubility relationships is a common source of error. Understanding the underlying thermodynamics and recognizing exceptions prevents mistakes.

classifications.type: Error: Assuming All Solids Follow the General Rule
classifications.value: Applying 'solubility increases with temperature' to all solids without checking $\Delta H_{soln}$
classifications.behavior: Always verify the sign of $\Delta H_{soln}$ or consult solubility data before predicting temperature effects

examples.explanation: Wrong: 'Heating will dissolve more $\text{Ca(OH)}_2$'; Correct: heating causes precipitation

classifications.type: Error: Confusing Gas and Solid Behavior
classifications.value: Applying solid-solubility rules to gases or vice versa
classifications.behavior: Remember: solids (mostly) ↑ solubility with ↑ $T$; gases ↓ solubility with ↑ $T$

examples.explanation: Wrong: 'Refrigeration decreases $\text{CO}_2$ solubility'; Correct: refrigeration increases $\text{CO}_2$ solubility

classifications.value: Focusing only on equilibrium solubility while neglecting dissolution/crystallization rates
classifications.behavior: Consider both equilibrium solubility and kinetic factors (diffusion, nucleation) for real-world predictions

examples.explanation: Higher temperature increases both solubility (equilibrium) and dissolution rate (kinetics); both contribute to faster sweetening

classifications.value: Reading solubility values incorrectly from graphs or tables
characteristics: Unit errors (g/100 g vs. g/100 mL)
classifications.behavior: Check units carefully; note phase transitions; interpolate linearly only over small temperature ranges

examples.explanation: Solubility peaks at $32.4^\circ\text{C}$; linear interpolation across this point gives incorrect values

worked_examples.title: Error Analysis: Gas vs. Solid Solubility
worked_examples.problem: A student claims that heating a saturated $\text{CO}_2$ solution will allow more $\text{CO}_2$ to dissolve, by analogy with sugar in water. Identify and correct the error.

step_by_step.explanation: $\text{CO}_2$ is a gas; sugar is a solid
step_by_step.calculation: Different solute types have different temperature-solubility relationships

step_by_step.explanation: Most solids: solubility ↑ with ↑ $T$; gases: solubility ↓ with ↑ $T$
step_by_step.calculation: $\text{CO}_2$ follows gas behavior

step_by_step.explanation: Solid dissolution is typically endothermic; gas dissolution is exothermic
step_by_step.calculation: $\text{CO}_2(g) \rightleftharpoons \text{CO}_2(aq) + \text{heat}$; heating shifts equilibrium left

step_by_step.explanation: Heating a saturated $\text{CO}_2$ solution causes $\text{CO}_2$ to escape, not dissolve further
step_by_step.calculation: Correct: heating decreases $\text{CO}_2$ solubility

worked_examples.final_answer: Error: applied solid-solubility rule to a gas; correct: heating decreases $\text{CO}_2$ solubility
worked_examples.concept_applied: Gas and solid solubilities respond oppositely to temperature changes.

worked_examples.title: Problem-Solving Strategy: Temperature-Solubility Analysis Checklist
worked_examples.problem: Outline a systematic approach for predicting temperature effects on solubility.

step_by_step.explanation: Determine if solute is solid, liquid, or gas; note solvent properties
Is dissolution endothermic ($\Delta H > 0$) or exothermic ($\Delta H < 0$)? Consult data if unknown
step_by_step.calculation: Most solids: $\Delta H > 0$; most gases: $\Delta H < 0$

step_by_step.explanation: Increasing temperature favors the endothermic direction
step_by_step.calculation: If $\Delta H_{soln} > 0$: ↑ $T$ → ↑ solubility; If $\Delta H_{soln} < 0$: ↑ $T$ → ↓ solubility

step_by_step.step: Step 4: Check for exceptions or transitions
step_by_step.explanation: Consult solubility curves for non-monotonic behavior or phase changes
step_by_step.calculation: Note hydrate transitions, solubility maxima/minima

step_by_step.explanation: Higher temperature increases dissolution/crystallization rates
step_by_step.calculation: Equilibrium solubility vs. rate of reaching equilibrium

step_by_step.step: Step 6: Verify with data or experiment
step_by_step.explanation: When possible, confirm predictions with solubility tables or simple tests
step_by_step.calculation: Empirical verification strengthens confidence in prediction

worked_examples.final_answer: Follow 6-step checklist: identify solute, determine $\Delta H$, apply Le Chatelier, check exceptions, consider kinetics, verify
worked_examples.concept_applied: Systematic analysis prevents misapplication of temperature-solubility rules.

sections.introduction: Test your understanding with these practice problems. Try solving before looking at solutions.

key_concept.definition: Practice problems reinforce temperature-solubility concepts for both solids and gases.
key_concept.context: Cover solutions and attempt each problem independently, then check your work.

worked_examples.title: Practice Problem 1
worked_examples.problem: The solubility of $\text{KCl}$ is $34.0$ g/100 g water at $20^\circ\text{C}$ and $56.7$ g/100 g water at $100^\circ\text{C}$. If you have a saturated solution at $100^\circ\text{C}$ with $56.7$ g $\text{KCl}$ in $100$ g water, how much $\text{KCl}$ will crystallize when cooled to $20^\circ\text{C}$?

step_by_step.explanation: Initial (100°C): 56.7 g/100 g water; Final (20°C): 34.0 g/100 g water
Excess beyond final solubility crystallizes
step_by_step.calculation: $\text{Crystallized} = 56.7 \text{ g} - 34.0 \text{ g} = 22.7 \text{ g}$

worked_examples.concept_applied: Cooling a saturated solution of a solid with positive $\Delta H_{soln}$ causes crystallization.

worked_examples.title: Practice Problem 2
worked_examples.problem: Explain why fish are more likely to experience oxygen stress in warm water than in cold water, using solubility principles.

step_by_step.explanation: Dissolved oxygen ($\text{O}_2$) is essential for fish respiration
Gas solubility decreases with increasing temperature
step_by_step.calculation: Warm water holds less dissolved $\text{O}_2$ than cold water at the same pressure

step_by_step.explanation: Lower dissolved $\text{O}_2$ reduces oxygen available for fish; metabolic rates also increase with temperature, raising oxygen demand
step_by_step.calculation: Double stress: less supply + higher demand

worked_examples.final_answer: Warm water holds less dissolved oxygen (lower solubility) while fish metabolic rates increase, creating oxygen stress
worked_examples.concept_applied: Gas solubility decreases with temperature; biological systems are sensitive to dissolved gas concentrations.

worked_examples.title: Practice Problem 3
worked_examples.problem: A saturated solution of $\text{Ca(OH)}_2$ at $20^\circ\text{C}$ contains $0.165$ g/100 g water. Predict what happens when the solution is heated to $80^\circ\text{C}$, given that $\text{Ca(OH)}_2$ solubility decreases with temperature.

step_by_step.explanation: $\text{Ca(OH)}_2$ dissolution is exothermic; solubility decreases with increasing temperature
Heating shifts equilibrium toward solid; excess solute crystallizes
step_by_step.calculation: Precipitate of $\text{Ca(OH)}_2$ will form

worked_examples.final_answer: Heating causes $\text{Ca(OH)}_2$ to precipitate because its solubility decreases with temperature
worked_examples.concept_applied: Exceptions to the general rule occur for exothermic dissolution processes.

worked_examples.title: Practice Problem 4
worked_examples.problem: Why is recrystallization an effective purification method for $\text{KNO}_3$ but not for $\text{NaCl}$?

step_by_step.explanation: $\text{KNO}_3$: large solubility increase with temperature; $\text{NaCl}$: small solubility increase
Large solubility change allows significant crystallization upon cooling; small change yields little purification
step_by_step.calculation: $\text{KNO}_3$: high recovery of pure crystals; $\text{NaCl}$: most solute remains dissolved

worked_examples.final_answer: $\text{KNO}_3$ has strong temperature-dependent solubility, enabling effective recrystallization; $\text{NaCl}$ has weak dependence, making recrystallization inefficient
worked_examples.concept_applied: Recrystallization effectiveness depends on the magnitude of solubility change with temperature.

worked_examples.title: Practice Problem 5
worked_examples.problem: A carbonated beverage is bottled at $5^\circ\text{C}$ under $3$ atm $\text{CO}_2$ pressure. If the bottle is opened at $25^\circ\text{C}$, why does it fizz more vigorously than if opened at $5^\circ\text{C}$?

step_by_step.explanation: Gas solubility depends on both temperature and pressure (Henry's law)
step_by_step.calculation: $C = k_H \times P$; $k_H$ increases with $T$ for gases

step_by_step.explanation: At higher temperature, $\text{CO}_2$ solubility is lower at the same pressure
step_by_step.calculation: At $25^\circ\text{C}$, equilibrium $\text{CO}_2$ concentration is lower than at $5^\circ\text{C}$

step_by_step.explanation: Opening the bottle drops pressure to 1 atm; solution is now supersaturated with $\text{CO}_2$
step_by_step.calculation: Degree of supersaturation is greater at $25^\circ\text{C}$ because equilibrium concentration is lower

step_by_step.explanation: Greater supersaturation + faster kinetics at higher temperature → more vigorous fizzing
step_by_step.calculation: Both equilibrium and kinetic factors favor rapid $\text{CO}_2$ release at $25^\circ\text{C}$

worked_examples.final_answer: At $25^\circ\text{C}$, $\text{CO}_2$ solubility is lower and kinetics are faster, causing more vigorous fizzing upon opening
worked_examples.concept_applied: Temperature affects both equilibrium solubility and kinetic rates for gas dissolution.

summary: Temperature affects solubility differently for solids and gases due to the thermodynamics of dissolution. For most solid solutes, dissolution is endothermic ($\Delta H_{soln} > 0$), so solubility increases with increasing temperature (Le Chatelier's principle: heat acts as a reactant). This relationship enables recrystallization purification and supersaturated solution preparation. For gases dissolving in liquids, dissolution is exothermic ($\Delta H_{soln} < 0$), so solubility decreases with increasing temperature (heat is a product; adding heat shifts equilibrium toward gas phase). This explains why warm soda goes flat faster and why thermal pollution reduces dissolved oxygen in aquatic ecosystems. Exceptions exist: some solids like $\text{Ca(OH)}_2$ have exothermic dissolution and decreased solubility with increasing temperature; $\text{Na}_2\text{SO}_4$ exhibits a solubility maximum due to hydrate transitions. The van't Hoff equation quantifies temperature dependence: $\ln(S_2/S_1) = -(\Delta H_{soln}/R)(1/T_2 - 1/T_1)$. Henry's law ($C = k_H \times P$) describes gas solubility, with $k_H$ increasing with temperature for most gases. Common errors include: applying solid-solubility rules to gases, ignoring exceptions like $\text{Ca(OH)}_2$, and neglecting kinetic effects. A systematic 6-step analysis (identify solute, determine $\Delta H$, apply Le Chatelier, check exceptions, consider kinetics, verify) ensures accurate predictions. Mastery of temperature-solubility relationships is essential for laboratory purification techniques, industrial process design, environmental management, and understanding everyday phenomena like carbonated beverages and aquatic ecosystem health.
